coredata.dc:title: Gold nanoparticles stabilized by graphene quantum dots as catalysts for CC bond cleavage in β-O-4 lignin model compounds
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Fangwei
Zhang, Jiali
Guo, Shouwu
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we report oxidative cleavage of CC bonds in β-O-4 lignin models that is catalyzed by gold nanoparticles stabilized by graphene quantum dots (AuNPs-GQDs). We demonstrate that GQDs can enhance the catalytic activity of AuNPs. The AuNPs-GQDs shows the highest catalytic activity when the mass ratio of AuNPs to GQDs is of 1:18. Moreover, AuNPs-GQDs exhibits higher activity in the cleavage of Cα Cβ bond of the compounds when their Cβ is adjacent to an oxygen atom. The possible oxidation pathway is proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Lignin
Gold nanoparticles
Cleavage
Catalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of graphene quantum dots stabilized gold nanoparticles (AuNPs-GQDs)
General procedure for the catalytic oxidation of β-O-4 lignin model compounds

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed oxidative cleavage of β-O-4 lignin model compounds by AuNPs-GQDs18.
Oxidation of BO41one catalyzed by bare GQDs. Conditions: 0.1 mg of BO41one, 0.01 mg of GQDs in 0.5 mL phosphate buffer (0.05 mol L−1, pH 7), 1 h. The conversion was 38%.
Catalytic activity of the AuNPs-GQDs with different ratio of AuNPs to GQDs in the BO41ol oxidation.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Conditions: 0.41 μmol of BO41ol, 0.01 mg of AuNPs (12.5 mol%), 17.6 μmol of H2O2 in 0.5 mL 0.05 mol L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 7), 75 °C, 4 h.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Catalytic activity of AuNPs-GQDs18 in oxidation of small alcohols and β-O-4 lignin model compounds.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Conditions: 0.41 μmol of substrate, 0.19 mg of AuNPs-GQDs18, 17.6 μmol of H2O2 in 0.5 mL 0.05 mol L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 7), 75 °C, 4 h. The conversion of the alcohols (black), yield of ketone intermediates (blue) and yield of the acid products (red) were marked respectively under substrates. [a] The reaction was carried out at 50 °C. [b] The product was 3,4-dimethoxybenzoic acid (veratric acid).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Catalytic activity of various catalysts in further oxidation of BO41one.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Conditions: 0.41 μmol of substrate, 0.01 mg of carbon material. Phosphate buffer 0.05 mol L−1 (pH 7), 75 °C, 1 h.

head.ce:title: Gold nanoparticles stabilized by graphene quantum dots as catalysts for CC bond cleavage in β-O-4 lignin model compounds

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronic Engineering, School of Electronic Information and Electrical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronic Information and Electrical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we report oxidative cleavage of CC bonds in β-O-4 lignin models that is catalyzed by gold nanoparticles stabilized by graphene quantum dots (AuNPs-GQDs). We demonstrate that GQDs can enhance the catalytic activity of AuNPs. The AuNPs-GQDs shows the highest catalytic activity when the mass ratio of AuNPs to GQDs is of 1:18. Moreover, AuNPs-GQDs exhibits higher activity in the cleavage of Cα Cβ bond of the compounds when their Cβ is adjacent to an oxygen atom. The possible oxidation pathway is proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: Lignin, a natural polymer that plays an important role in plants' lives, can be converted to biofuels or other valuable chemicals by pyrolysis [1]. One of the key steps for lignin degeneration is to cleave the β-O-4 linkage, an interconnecting bond in native lignin [2–4]. To accelerate the cleavage of β-O-4 linkage, numerous catalysts have been developed [5–14], which include different types of catalysts [15,16], mainly involving Mn (III) [17], Cu (I/II) [18–22], VO [23–27] and metal free catalysts [28–31]. The catalytic oxidation reactions of β-O-4 linkage are accomplished through oxidation of the Cα OH and the cleavage of C1 Cα, Cα Cβ, or Cβ O bonds (Fig. S1). However, the oxidation of the Cα OH to Cα O in β-O-4 alcohol increases the Cα Cβ bond energy, making the Cα Cβ bond harder to be converted [24]. Thus, different catalyst systems have been used for the cleavage of Cα Cβ in β-O-4 model compounds. For example, Crocker et al. reported that the Cα OH in the model compound was first oxidized to CO, then the Cα Cβ bond in lignin β-O-4 was selectively cleaved by using TPPFeCl/tBuOOH or DDQ/NaNO2 or TEMPO/NaNO2 systems [32]. An efficient and selective strategy for the cleavage of Cα Cβ bond in lignin model compounds using a Cu/1,10-phenanthroline catalyst was also developed [24]. The Cα Cβ bond cleavage of β-O-4 linkage in lignin model compounds can also be achieved using metal-free homogeneous catalyst that consists of 4-acetamido-TEMP in combination with HNO3 and HCl [30]. Further, a one-pot oxidation cleavage of β-O-4 linkage using Pd/CeO2 as a heterogeneous catalyst was also reported, in which the Cα OH of 2-phenoxy-1-phenylethanol (PP-ol) was oxidized to 2-phenoxy-1-phenylethanone with PdNPs catalyst, and at the same time, the oxidation cleavage of Cα Cβ bond was catalyzed by CeO2 [33]. Recently, several copper complexes as catalysts for one-pot conversion of β-O-4 linkage were also reported [21]. Generally, the cleavage of Cα Cβ bond in β-O-4 linkages of lignin model compounds with catalysts usually requires the use of organic solvents and/or high temperature.

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) has been widely used as catalyst for selective oxidation of benzyl alcohols to the corresponding aldehydes, acetones or acids in aqueous solution under mild conditions [34,35]. Compared to AuNPs, supported AuNPs, like Au/CeO2, and Au/C, have also been used to catalyze oxidation of some monoaromatic lignin model compounds, such as 3,4-dimethoxyphenyl methanol (veratryl alcohol) [36,37] and 1-phenylethane-1,2-diol [38,39]. Recently, the oxidation of β-O-4 lignin model compounds with AuNPs supported SiO2 or Li-Al LDH was reported [40,41]. Previously, nanocomposites of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and other materials were used for electro- [42–44], photon- [45–52] and thermo- [37,53–57] catalysis owing to the good chemical/physical stabilities of GQDs. For instance, we have demonstrated that composites of AuNPs and GQDs can catalyze the selective oxidation of veratryl alcohol to veratraldehyde or veratric acid [37]. In this work, we explore the cleavage of Cα Cβ in β-O-4 lignin model compounds to corresponding acids using GQDs stabilized gold nanoparticles (AuNPs-GQDs) in aqueous solution.

(DL)-1-phenylethanol, 1-phenylethane-1,2-diol, diphenylmethanol, HAuCl4 and BO43ol were purchased and used as received. (Table S4) Preparations and characterizations of 2-methyl-1-phenylpropan-1-ol, 1,2-diphenylethan-1-ol, 2-(4-methoxyphenoxy)-1-phenylethan-1-one (BO41one), 2-(4-methoxyphenoxy)-1-phenylethan-1-ol (BO41ol), 2-phenoxy-1-phenylethan-1-one (BO42one) and 2-phenoxy-1-phenylethan-1-ol (BO42ol) are summarized in supporting information (SI). The GQDs were prepared through the photon-Fenton reaction of graphene oxide sheets as described in our previous work [58].

AuNPs is prepared by reducing HAuCl4 and sodium citrate (Na3CA) solution with NaBH4 according to literature [59,60]. In a typical experiment, 20 mg of HAuCl4 (58.9 μmol) and 50 mg of Na3CA∙2H2O (170 μmol) was dissolved in 200 mL distilled water in a flask. The solution was stirred for 15 min at 35–40 °C. Then, 10 mL of cold NaBH4 (52.9 mmol L−1) was introduced. The reaction mixture was stirred for 45 min. The magnet stirrer was removed, and another 22 mL distilled water was added into the solution. The as-formed gold nanoparticle (AuNPs) colloid (0.050 g L−1) was kept at 8 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of graphene quantum dots stabilized gold nanoparticles (AuNPs-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: AuNPs-GQDs was prepared by simply mixing them two. Briefly, the GQDs solution (1 mL, 7.2 g L−1) was neutralized by NaOH (1.6 mL, 10 mmol L−1), then injected to the AuNPs colloid (8 mL, 0.050 g L−1). The mixture was placed in a horizonal oscillator and kept at 50 °C for 30 min. The as-prepared mixture is labeled as AuNPs-GQDs18 (mAuNPs:mGQDs = 1:18). For comparison, AuNPs-GQDs9 and AuNPs-GQDs27 were prepared under the same condition, but different mass ratios of AuNPs to GQDs.

The AuNPs/C (1%) catalyst was prepared according to literature [61]. In a typical experiment, 10 mg of activated carbon was dispersed in 1 mL of distilled water. Then, 2 mL of AuNPs colloid (0.050 mg mL−1) was injected into the dispersion. The mixture was kept in a turn-over mixer (120 rpm) for 15 min. The AuNPs/C catalyst was then collected and washed by water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for the catalytic oxidation of β-O-4 lignin model compounds
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical reaction, 125 μL phosphate buffer (0.2 mol L−1, pH 7), 90 μL distilled water, 265 μL AuNPs-GQDs18 (0.72 g L−1, 0.01 mg gold) were mixed. Then, 0.1 mg substrate in 50 μL ethanol was dried at 50 °C in an open bottle. The bottle was sealed after the AuNPs-GQDs18 solution and 20 μL fresh H2O2 (30 g L−1) was injected into it. The reaction mixture was heated at 75 °C for 4 h in a horizonal oscillator. The conversion and yield were determined by HPLC. The catalyst could be recovered by dialysis (8000–140,000 Da) and reused for 2 times (see Table S3).

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image was acquired in tapping mode with a Multimode Nanoscope V scanning probe microscopy system (Bruker, USA), using AN-NSC 10 AFM cantilever tip (SHNIT Co., Russia). The specimen was prepared by solution casting the aqueous suspensions of GQDs on cleaved mica surface. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained using a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) operated at 200 kV. The samples were prepared by solution casting the aqueous suspensions of AuNPs or AuNPs-GQDs on ultrathin carbon films with copper grid support and drying under ambient condition in a dryer. The 1HNMR spectra were acquired with an Avance III600 MHz system (Bruker, Germany). HPLC data were collected on an 1220 Infinity LC system (Agilent, USA) with a ZORBAX Bonus-RP column (4.6 × 250 mm, 5 μm filling. Agilent, USA) at 32 °C.

The GQDs used in the work was prepared through the photo-Fenton reaction of graphene oxide [58], and its morphology was characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Fig. S2). The average lateral size of the GQDs was <10 nm [62], and the height is ~1 nm, revealing the single atomic layered feature [60,63]. AuNPs was prepared through the reduction of tetrachloroauric acid with sodium citrate and characterized (Fig. S3a), then mixed with the GQDs solution at certain weight ratios that resulting in the GQDs stabilized AuNPs (AuNPs-GQDs). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of AuNPs-GQDs is shown in Fig. S3b. The hyperfine structure of AuNPs-GQDs is further confirmed by HRTEM image, the crystalline lattice constant is 0.230 nm and 0.235 nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite and the (111) facet of gold (Fig. S3c), respectively. The GQDs possibly attaches to the AuNPs through weak interactions [59,62].

To investigate their catalytic activities, the AuNPs-GQDs with different GQDs to AuNPs ratios were prepared as described in the experimental section. The catalysis of the AuNPs-GQDs was evaluated with a β-O-4 lignin model compound, 2-(4-methoxyphenoxy)-1-phenylethan-1-ol (BO41ol). The reaction was conducted with H2O2 as an oxidant in phosphate buffer (pH 7) at 75 °C. The reaction temperature was actually explored systematically, and it is found that the conversion of BO41ol increased dramatically from 25.0 to 100%, and the yield of BA also increased from 8.4 to 73%, while the yield of the intermediate 2-(4-methoxyphenoxy)-1-phenylethan-1-one (BO41one) disappeared when the reaction temperature is 75 °C (Fig. S4). Higher temperature facilitates the further oxidation of BO41one to BA. When the temperature was further up to 100 °C, the conversion of BO41ol remained, while the yield of BA decreased, possibly due to the formation of more byproducts by free radical reactions [64].

The final reaction product benzoic acid (BA) was detected by HPLC. As summarized in Table 1 , if the individual GQDs and AuNPs were used as catalyst, the yields were 3.2 and 13%, respectively. When the AuNPs-GQDs with AuNPs to GQDs ratio of 5.3% was used, the yield reached to 73%. This suggests that GQDs improved the catalytic performance of AuNPs. Similar result was also reported by others [44]. Since the activity of AuNPs-GQDs was not a simple addition of the two components, we thus propose that the certain interact should be existed between GQDs and AuNPs. To illustrate this, AuNPs and AuNPs-GQDs were conditioned separately at 75 °C for 4 h in 0.05 mol L−1 phosphate buffer (pH 7). As shown in Fig. S5, the surface plasmon resonance peak was still observable from the UV–Vis spectrum of AuNPs-GQDs after conditioning, but not from the AuNPs, indicating that the GQDs should somehow stabilize the AuNPs. When the weight ratio of GQDs to AuNPs increased from 0 to 18:1, the yield of BA raised from 13% to 73%, but, once it is further lifted to 27:1, the yield of BA dropped to 57%. This may be caused by the effect of the excessive GQDs to the diffusion of the reactants to AuNPs surface. On the other hand, the consumption of H2O2 increased from 14 to 72% with an increase of ratio of GQDs in AuNPs-GQDs, implying that excess amount of GQDs could also promote self-decomposition of H2O2 [53,65], besides effects on the reaction. Thus, the ratio of AuNPs to GQDs used in the following experiments was 1:18 (5.3 wt% of AuNPs), namely AuNPs-GQDs18. Moreover, the catalytic performances of AuNPs-GQDs18 and conventional AuNPs/C (1% of gold) were compared in Table S1. It can be found that the yield with the AuNPs/C catalyst is only 48% of BA, much lower than that using AuNPs-GQDs18, meaning that existence of GQDs is more favorable to oxidation of BO41ol than carbon.

The catalytic activity of AuNPs-GQDs18 was also tested for the oxidation of 1-phenylethan-1-ol, 2-methyl-1-phenylpropan-1-ol, diphenylmethanol, 1,2-diphenylethan-1-ol, 1-phenylethane-1,2-diol and three β-O-4 lignin models, and the results are summarized in Table 2 . Generally, AuNPs-GQDs18 can catalyze the oxidization of all these compounds but show different selectivity. The substrates 1-phenylethan-1-ol (Entry 1), 2-methyl-1-phenylpropan-1-ol (Entry 2), and diphenylmethanol (Entry 3) were oxidized to the ketone with yield of 98%, 98% and 61%, respectively, and almost no BA was detected. Compounds 1-phenylethane-1,2-diol (Entry 4), BO42ol (Entry 6), BO41ol (Entry 7) could be oxidized to the corresponding acid with yields above 70%, which are higher than that of 1,2-diphenylethan-1-ol (Entry 5). As for BO43ol (Entry 8), both ketone and acid were found as main products. Apparently, the cleavage of Cα Cβ in these β-O-4 lignin model compounds takes place following a similar Baeyer-Villiger reaction mechanism [66]. However, the detailed reaction mechanisms for the lignin model compounds in which the Cβ attaches to an oxygen atom (Cβ O) seem different from the traditional Baeyer-Villiger reaction. Nevertheless, it is obvious that oxygen adjacent to Cβ is favorable for the cleavage of Cα Cβ. For instance, BO42ol (Entry 6) and BO41ol (Entry 7) were converted to BA with higher yield, in comparison with 2-methyl-1-phenylpropan-1-ol (Entry 2) and 1,2-diphenylethan-1-ol (Entry 5). The reason may be that when the β-O-4 lignin model compound is oxidized to ketone, the Cβ is more positively charged, the hydrolysis reaction might occur at the Cβ forming Cβ OH, which is highly active and then transform to BA eventually (Entry 7) [38], as shown in Scheme 1 . This assumption is supported by the presence of intermediate 2-hydroxy-1-phenylethan-1-one (~0.5%) during the reaction. This pathway is more likely to happen in the presence of GQDs.

Actually, as shown in Table S2, bare GQDs can catalyze the hydrolysis of the BO41one intermediate at pH 7. The reason may be raised from the π-π stack interaction between the ketone intermediate and the GQDs that accelerate the reaction, a similar phenomenon as we've observed in the cleavage of DNA molecular via GQDs-Cu hybrid [37,58]. The detected products of this reaction are shown in Scheme 2 . GQDs show higher activity than H2SO4 and some other carbon materials in the reaction (Table 3 ). Overall, in the presence of GQDs, AuNPs can effectively catalyze the cleavage of the Cα Cβ bond in β-O-4 lignin model compound, though the exact effects of GQDs and the detailed catalytic mechanisms of AuNPs-GQDs18 to different lignin model compounds have not been fully understood for us at the moment, yet. However, it is worth noting that with AuNPs-GQDs catalyst the oxidative cleavage of β-O-4 linkage can take place more efficiently under even mild conditions in comparing other catalytic reaction systems. For example, Santos et al. found that the Cα OH oxidation of a β-O-4 lignin model compounds can be catalyzed with AuNP/SiO2 at merely 40 °C [40], but to further cleave the CC bond another Se based catalyst had to be utilized. Later on, Song et al. designed AuNP/Li-Al LDH catalyst for lignin and lignin model oxidative cleavage and illustrated that the β-O-4 lignin model compound could readily be oxidized to the corresponding ketone at 120 °C for 8 h, but after another 40 h, only ~10% of the ketone was further oxidized to achieve the Cα Cβ cleavage [41]. Actually, so far, most of the catalyst systems used for the oxidative cleavage of β-O-4 linkage require hash conditions [21,24,27,67–70].

In summary, we demonstrate that AuNPs-GQDs can catalyze the cleavage of Cα Cβ bond in β-O-4 lignin model compounds. AuNPs-GQDs show higher catalytic activity than individual GQDs or AuNPs, possibly due to the stability of AuNPs that is improved in the presence of GQDs. Additionally, GQDs can also catalyze the further oxidation of the ketone intermediate. We also found that efficiency of the Cα Cβ cleavage is dramatically high when an oxygen atom is next to Cβ (Cβ O) in the lignin model compounds.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was carried out with financial support from the National “973 Program” of China (No. 2015CB931801).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The Supporting Information is available free of charge via the Internet. Preparation and characterization data for AuNPs-GQDs and alcohol substrates were included. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2019.03.024.
